sections.heading: Modern methods grew while overall use stayed almost unchanged
sections.body: Among all women aged 15 to 49, contraceptive use was nearly flat at 33.8 percent in 2025, compared with 34.1 percent in 2022. The more meaningful movement was inside the total: modern method use rose from 24.3 to 25.7 percent, while traditional method use fell from 9.8 to 8.1 percent. The national picture is therefore less about more women using contraception overall and more about a shift in the mix of methods counted among current users.

sections.heading: The regional spread is wide enough to change the story
sections.body: Davao Region had the highest share of all women using any contraceptive method at 45.1 percent, while BARMM was lowest at 16.2 percent. Central Visayas was also near the low end at 25.7 percent, and NCR was below the national level at 28.5 percent. That range is much wider than the national change between 2022 and 2025, so the regional pattern is one of the most important ways to read the data.

sections.heading: High-use regions are mostly high-modern-method regions
sections.body: The regions with relatively high overall use also tend to have high modern method use. Davao Region led both any-method use at 45.1 percent and modern method use at 37.1 percent, followed closely on the modern measure by MIMAROPA at 33.9 percent, SOCCSKSARGEN at 32.5 percent, and Caraga at 32.2 percent. This matters because regional differences are not just about whether contraception is used, but about whether use is anchored mainly in modern methods.

sections.heading: Several regions moved in opposite directions since 2022
sections.body: Central Visayas had the largest decline in any-method use, falling from 37.1 percent in 2022 to 25.7 percent in 2025. Cagayan Valley also declined sharply, from 40.3 to 32.9 percent, while BARMM fell from 21.4 to 16.2 percent. In the other direction, Eastern Visayas, SOCCSKSARGEN, and Caraga each rose by 4.0 percentage points, while MIMAROPA rose by 3.9 points and Davao Region by 3.8 points.

sections.heading: Traditional use changes the regional texture, not the national headline
sections.body: Traditional method use was 8.1 percent nationally, but some regions sit clearly above that level. Eastern Visayas was highest at 12.1 percent, followed by Ilocos Region at 11.8 percent and Western Visayas at 10.8 percent. Zamboanga Peninsula¹ was different: any-method use was below the national level at 30.2 percent, but most of that use was modern methods, with 24.7 percent modern and 5.6 percent traditional.

sections.heading: Use is concentrated in the middle reproductive ages
sections.body: Contraceptive use among all women rises sharply after the youngest ages and peaks in the 30s. It was 3.1 percent among women 15 to 19 and 19.9 percent among women 20 to 24, then reached 49.5 percent at ages 30 to 34 and 53.9 percent at ages 35 to 39. By ages 40 to 44 it remained high at 51.1 percent, before easing to 39.3 percent among women 45 to 49.

sections.heading: Family-building stage shapes the all-women pattern
sections.body: The number of living children is one of the sharpest non-regional divides in the data. Any-method use was only 4.2 percent among women with no living children, but it rose to 52.6 percent among women with one or two children and 66.8 percent among women with three or four children. The mix also shifts with family size: women with three or four children had 55.8 percent modern method use, compared with 2.4 percent among women with no living children.

sections.heading: Rural women had higher use, but the gap was modest
sections.body: Rural women had higher contraceptive use than urban women in 2025, 36.3 percent compared with 31.9 percent. The difference was larger for modern methods, with rural women at 28.8 percent and urban women at 23.3 percent. Still, the 4.4-point urban-rural gap in any-method use is much smaller than the spread across regions, which suggests that place matters more at the regional level than through residence alone.

sections.heading: Education differences do not run in a simple ladder
sections.body: Contraceptive use was highest among women with Grades 1-6 education at 47.9 percent and also high among women with post-secondary education at 45.9 percent. It was much lower among women with Grades 11-12 education at 15.2 percent and among women with no education at 24.2 percent. Because this is an all-women denominator, these education differences should be read alongside age and family-building stage rather than as a simple ranking of advantage.

sections.heading: The wealth gradient is visible but not the whole story
sections.body: Any-method use generally declined as household wealth rose, from 39.6 percent in the lowest quintile to 28.4 percent in the highest. Modern method use showed a clearer version of the same pattern, at 32.5 percent in the lowest quintile and 20.0 percent in the highest. The difference is meaningful, but it is still smaller than the gap between the highest- and lowest-use regions.

takeaways: National contraceptive use among all women was nearly unchanged from 2022, but the method mix shifted toward modern methods.
Regional variation is the strongest pattern in the data, with Davao Region far above BARMM and Central Visayas on any-method use.
Use is shaped by life stage as well as geography, rising sharply among women in their 30s and among women with living children.
footnotes: ¹ Includes the province of Sulu in 2025.
